section_title: INTRODUCTION TO HIGH REWARD TRADING SETUPS
content:
Welcome to the sixth teaching of Month Two of the ICT Mentorship, specifically dealing with "The Secrets
To High Reward Trading Setups."
Some of you may have already gone through my Trading Plan Development series, which was a long video
series with a lot of information, primarily aimed at individuals with no prior exposure to the marketplace,
guiding them on direction and focus.

section_title: THE NECESSITY OF FOUNDATION AND MENTORSHIP
content:
While the Trading Plan Development series is a great deal of information for a neophyte, it is necessary to
go through these things because it provides a framework or a foundation to build upon, giving your trading
career direction.
One of the most recurring themes in my role as a mentor is the question: 'Where do I begin? What should I
do first? Where should I be focusing my attention? What should I be studying?'

The majority of you are already exposed to my content and material related to trading, specifically foreign
exchange. If one were to look at what I actually do on a day-by-day basis, what is the procedure, what are
the things that I do to elect a specific stance on the marketplace?
What makes me bullish, what makes me bearish? Why do I focus on one currency pair over another? All
those things are decisions and processes.

section_title: A MICRO VERSION OF THE TRADING PLAN DEVELOPMENT SERIES
content:
It's important that we start with a micro version of the Trading Plan Development series in this teaching.
While it's not going to be as comprehensive as the full series, this one will be more specific and salient to
the things that I do as a specific forex trader.
I don't look at all the things the Trading Plan Development series video course spoke about. They do
occasionally come up in my thought processes, but they're not all required to come to a trade decision.

It's important that we go into this with the proper mindset. I don't want anybody thinking we're going into
trade signals, trading patterns, stop-loss placement, or trade management. None of those things are
important here.
This is the last primer for us before we start going into the specifics of breaking down individual decisions.
Each process, as a collective whole, defines the ICT mindset: what we do daily, what frames our opinion,
when we change opinions, and when we move to the sideline. All these things come by way of process
thinking.

While entry signals, stop placement, patterns, order blocks, and institutional order flow are exciting, a bit of
'dry information' is necessary. Don't think this video isn't teaching you anything; it truly is.
It's going to teach you how to think in terms of foundations because we have to understand where we're
building on to elect a decision relative to whether we're going to be a buyer or seller, or whether we're
going to stay on the sidelines. That's the real secret to trading: understanding what makes the process
arrive at a decision.

You need to know what asset class you should be trading and what specific pair, if we're dealing with
foreign exchange as we are in this teaching. All those things will be a lot more clear by having the thought
processes that I'm going to introduce here.

There has to be a specific order, a hierarchy to what it is that we do as a trader, what makes our decisions
incremental. What do we do first? What do we focus on first?
I've been a strong supporter of the notion that every trader needs to have patience. After patience, we have
to understand what defines trade environments, if the environments are conducive for trading right now.
This is a topic that is largely untaught by many educators. I do a lot of work with that in this mentorship, and
it will be spread across the entire mentorship; there isn't one specific teaching about when you shouldn't
trade. You'll learn by experience.

The next stage is to determine a trade parameter: what makes your trading a buy or a sell? What gives you
those notions to even take action? It's not simply, 'It's been going up for the last 60 minutes, so I'm going to
buy,' or 'It's going lower because of an interest rate announcement, so I'm going to trade on that.'
It has to be very defined, specific, binary: 'You do this, or you don't do this.' 'Do X, or do Y.' It has to be a
very black-or-white decision process.

If you don't have your trading plan or your perspective or process in determining whether you want to be a
trader (buying or selling) right now, or staying on the sidelines, it will create a huge vacuum where lots of
emotional, psychological, and impulsive trading will creep in.
If you don't have these binary thought processes and where you're specifically dealing with in terms of
decision-making, you won't have any structure. Without structure, without having a refined, clear trading
model (what defines your trading, what makes your trading model uniquely yours), you're going to struggle.
It doesn't matter who teaches you, what principle or discipline you trade with; it's going to be an impossible
endeavor if you do not become highly organized.

section_title: DEFINING EXECUTABLE CRITERIA AND FLOWCHART THINKING
content:
After a long period of time spent in front of charts and working with individuals (and myself as a trader, as
I'm always a student learning about myself as a trader, not so much about the market anymore), you'll learn
that this helps you refine your trade parameters.
Next, you need to know what makes your executable criteria what it is, what makes you the buyer or the
seller. Having those parameters defined isn't just simply 'I'm bullish right now.' If you're bullish, what would
you do to be a buyer? And what would make that buying scenario negated? What would change the tone in
the marketplace for you to either move to the sidelines or, if you have a stop-loss hit, do you still consider
that being a buying condition? These things have to be specific; they have to be highly refined. It's got to be
like a flowchart format; you have to go from one step to the next.

If it's not like that, you're going to be very emotional, very psychologically influenced by the things that you
see in the price action and, worse, by online media, forums, Twitter, Facebook, people talking to you at
work, and your friends that are traders. All those things are going to be influential to you, and it's going to
be detrimental to your performance as a trader.
So, we don't care what anybody else's opinion is. We don't care about their opinion of our trading model. In
fact, we're not really trying to share our trading model with anybody; it's a unique trading plan and trading
model for you. You're going to define that and refine it for yourself. That's going to be your graduation when
you know exactly what's going to be framing your trading model.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING TRADE VIABILITY AND LEARNING BY EXPERIENCE
content:
You'll have a lot of help along the way through the remaining months of this mentorship, but they have to be
defined by you. I can't force you to be a day trader, a short-term trader, or a swing trader; you'll know which
one that is for you by the end of this mentorship.
More importantly, you have to understand why the trade should pan out, understand what makes the trade
viable. It's not just simply 'I see a trading pattern here,' or 'I believe I'm bullish,' or 'I believe I'm bearish' in
the marketplace. There has to be a real understanding of why that scenario should take place. And largely,
this is going to come by experience. That experience comes by taking action in a demo account, and when
you do that, experience should be logged and kept for future reference. That's the only way you're really
going to learn. If you just go into a demo account, click on buy and sell, and you're just waiting to see the
outcome, wanting to attribute winning trades as being a good trader and ignoring losing trades, that doesn't
help you as a developing trader.

The real secrets to finding high reward trade setups are, number one, you have to know what it is
specifically you're looking for and where to find that information. It's crucial to understand that efficiency in
trading comes by way of process-oriented thinking, and it doesn't come by way of reactionary or impulsive
thinking, which also leads to rushing ahead into trade signals prematurely.
I could tell you, if we were all going to sit over my email box and look at some of the things that I got by way
of feedback, the recurring theme is, 'I want to see trades, I want to see entries, get me in and get me out'
that type of perspective. I understand that; I get that. But I can tell you, being from where you are right now
and where I am now in my understanding as a trader, that's not what you need to know right now. It doesn't
feel good to hear that; it feels like I'm leading you down the primrose lane or deferring something that you
think you need to have right now. That's not true.

What it is, is you have to develop a process-oriented thinking. That means I can show you order blocks, I
can show you breakers, I can show you institutional order flow returning back to a mitigation block. I can
show you examples of that. But until you understand the process behind why these things should be doing
what they're going to do, it's going to be of no help to you. It's going to feel like I'm demonstrating toys, or
showing you what I can do. That's not what this is all about.
It's to show you with an intimate experience on a day-by-day basis, a weekly basis, a teaching tutorial
basis, a theme generalized over the month, that builds on your total understanding. The folks that are
struggling right now are the folks that are really trying to be reactionary or impulsively thinking about what
they want to do right now. That's the hardest thing for traders to do when they first get involved with
learning. They have this insatiable desire; they have to be trading right now, they want to get in, they want
to take signals.

section_title: ABUNDANCE OF SIGNALS, LACK OF DEFINED SETUPS
content:
I spent the entire first month, September, on a day-by-day basis showing you that there are a plethora of
trading signals all the time. We were laser-guided precision; there's no reason for you to be feeling rushed.
October didn't change; the way price is being delivered, none of these concepts fell out of fad; it still works.
But I needed to show you in the first month that there's nothing that's going to hinder your ability to find
signals, because there's always a lot of them. The problem is going to be that you don't know what defines
the setups for you as a trader, and you're not going to have a process-oriented thinking that leads you to
high reward trade setups. It comes by experience; it comes by showing you conceptual ideas and a broad
- brush idea of breaking down what it is that we look for in the marketplace and where that information
resides. So, it's really important before we get into this that you focus on the fact that what I'm showing you
in this teaching, while it doesn't give you technical...
